items.question_text: Which of the three VFR flight plan operating areas in Oceanic FIRs permits operations based on a distance-from-shore limit rather than an altitude ceiling?
items.explanation: The Oakland FIR is unique among the three VFR operating areas: its limit is based on distance from shore (less than 100 NM seaward within controlled airspace), while the CTAs use an altitude ceiling (below FL 180). The all-FIR category applies below Oceanic CTAs.

choices.text: The Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs.
The Oakland FIR, limited to less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace.
All Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs, limited to 50 NM seaward.
All three areas use the same distance-from-shore limit.

items.question_text: A controller needs to report an oceanic error event. Which of the following IS a reportable category under JO 7210.632?
items.explanation: JO 7210.632 establishes reporting procedures for Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and SAO verification. Height errors are a reportable category. Speed deviations, radar ID failures, and CPDLC message errors are not among the five listed categories.

choices.text: Speed deviations.
CPDLC message errors.
Radar identification failures.
Height errors.

items.question_text: An ARTCC has a 2-minute revision to an oceanic flight's boundary estimate. Must this revision be forwarded to the receiving ATS facility?
items.explanation: ARTCCs must forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more to the appropriate ATS facility. A 2-minute revision falls below the mandatory reporting threshold and does not require forwarding.

choices.text: No, because any change to an estimate must be forwarded regardless of magnitude.
No, because all boundary crossing times must be coordinated regardless of revision size.
Yes, because only revisions of 3 minutes or more require forwarding.
Yes, but only if the receiving facility has agreed to waive the forwarding requirement in an LOA.

items.question_text: Two oceanic facilities have installed AIDC hardware. One facility manager wants to begin using AIDC for coordination immediately. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: AIDC use for ATC coordination requires three elements: the capability must be implemented, a regional Interface Control Document must govern its use, and a letter of agreement must support it between the facilities. Hardware installation alone is insufficient without the governance documents.

choices.text: No, because AIDC use also requires a regional Interface Control Document and a letter of agreement between the facilities.
Yes, because AIDC capability has been implemented, which is the only prerequisite.
Yes, but only if the facility manager notifies the regional office within 24 hours.
No, AIDC may not be used for ATC coordination under any circumstances.

items.question_text: Two aircraft on reciprocal tracks have been vertically separated. Both report passing a significant point and the controller confirms they are now separated by the applicable same-direction longitudinal minimum. What may the controller do?
items.explanation: Vertical separation on reciprocal tracks may be discontinued when both aircraft have reported passing a significant point AND they are separated by at least the applicable minimum for same-direction longitudinal spacing. The second condition — confirmed longitudinal separation — is required before vertical separation is dropped.

choices.text: Discontinue vertical separation, because both aircraft have reported passing a significant point.
Discontinue all separation, because reciprocal-track aircraft have passed each other.
Discontinue vertical separation, because passing a significant point and achieving the same-direction minimum are the required conditions.
Maintain vertical separation for another 15 minutes regardless.

items.question_text: A controller working in the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace needs to determine the applicable lateral separation minimum for a flight path defined by Grid System waypoints. What minimum applies?
items.explanation: Within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace, a specific minimum of 12 NM applies between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints. This is a geographically specific rule that supersedes the general lateral separation minimum.

choices.text: 50 NM, the general oceanic lateral minimum.
23 NM, the standard reduced lateral minimum for RNP-approved aircraft.
10 NM, the standard en route radar separation minimum.
12 NM between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints.

items.question_text: Two aircraft are on intersecting flight paths. Aircraft A has 50 NM of protected airspace on each side. Aircraft B has 10 NM of protected airspace on each side. At what perpendicular distance from the flight path of the other aircraft does lateral separation exist?
items.explanation: For intersecting flight paths with constant but different width protected airspace, lateral separation exists when either aircraft is at or beyond a distance equal to the SUM of the protected airspace of both flight paths measured perpendicular to the other's flight path. Here, 50 + 10 = 60 NM.

choices.text: 60 NM, the sum of the protected airspace widths of both flight paths.
10 NM, the smaller of the two protected airspace widths.
50 NM, the larger of the two protected airspace widths.
30 NM, the average of the two protected airspace widths.

items.question_text: A controller is working a route at FL250 that has been satisfactorily flight checked with notice given to users. What is the maximum reduction in route protected airspace width permitted at this altitude before it begins widening?
items.explanation: At and above FL240, the width of protected airspace may be reduced to 10 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, before increasing on a 5-degree angle. Below FL240, the reduced width is 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles.

choices.text: 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.
15 miles on each side to 150 miles from the NAVAID.
No reduction is permitted at FL250.

items.question_text: Why must control action be taken when vertical separation is applied between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic boundary below FL 180?
items.explanation: Below FL 180, aircraft may be using local altimeter settings (QNH) on the offshore side and standard settings (QNE, 29.92) on the oceanic side. The difference between these settings can erode vertical separation if not accounted for.

choices.text: Because aircraft below FL 180 cannot maintain assigned altitudes in oceanic airspace.
Because vertical separation is never valid when aircraft cross any airspace boundary.
Because radar coverage is always lost at the offshore/oceanic boundary.
Because differences between standard altimeter setting (QNE) and local altimeter setting (QNH) could compromise the separation.

items.question_text: A controller chooses to separate an aircraft from a temporary stationary airspace reservation by vertical separation. How is the protected airspace around the reservation defined for this purpose?
items.explanation: When vertical separation is used to separate aircraft from a temporary stationary reservation, the protected geographical area is the reservation PLUS a buffer around the perimeter equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum. This buffer accounts for navigation uncertainty around the reservation boundary.

choices.text: The stationary reservation area plus a buffer of one-half the lateral separation minimum around the perimeter.
The stationary reservation area only, with no buffer.
The stationary reservation area plus a buffer of the full lateral separation minimum around the perimeter.
A cylinder of 5 NM radius centered on the reservation's geographic coordinates.

items.question_text: How does the longitudinal separation method for a temporary moving airspace reservation differ from the lateral method for the same reservation type?
items.explanation: For temporary moving reservations, longitudinal separation places the applicable minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation, treating the reservation as a moving block. Lateral separation ensures the aircraft's protected airspace does not overlap the time-dependent geographical area of the moving reservation. The third method, vertical, uses the reservation plus a perimeter buffer.

choices.text: Longitudinal is used only for stationary reservations; lateral is used only for moving reservations.
They are identical; the terms are interchangeable for moving reservations.
Longitudinal separation applies the minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation, while lateral separation keeps aircraft protected airspace clear of the geographic area.
Longitudinal separation requires a buffer of one-half the minimum; lateral does not.

items.question_text: When Section 7 North Atlantic procedures differ from the general Chapter 8 procedures, which procedures take precedence in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.explanation: The North Atlantic regional application rule states that ATC services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 7 except when noted otherwise. When Section 7 procedures differ from general Chapter 8, the Section 7 procedures control as the more specific regional rule.

choices.text: The general Chapter 8 procedures, because they apply uniformly throughout all oceanic airspace.
The procedures in Section 8, because the Caribbean and North Atlantic regions share the same rules.
Whichever set of procedures provides the larger separation minimum.
The Section 7 procedures, because they are the region-specific rules that govern the North Atlantic ICAO Region.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, which aircraft qualify for the 60 NM or 1-degree latitude lateral minimum?
items.explanation: The 60 NM / 1-degree minimum applies to two categories: (1) supersonic aircraft operating above FL 275, and (2) aircraft with MNPS or NAT HLA authorization operating within, transiting to/from, or operating for part of their flight within/above/below NAT HLA. The reduced minimum requires track-keeping capability.

choices.text: Nonturbojet aircraft operating in WATRS only.
All RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft regardless of supersonic capability.
Supersonic aircraft above FL 275, and aircraft with MNPS or NAT HLA authorization in specified operations.
Any aircraft with a Mode S transponder operating above FL 290.

items.question_text: How does the Caribbean ICAO Region's governing rule differ from the oceanic controlled airspace rule that allows directives and LOAs to supersede Chapter 8?
items.explanation: The Caribbean regional application (8-8-1) does not mention directives or LOAs as superseding authorities. It states that services are provided with the procedures and minima in the section except when noted otherwise. This differs from 8-1-1 which explicitly permits directives and LOAs to prescribe different procedures.

choices.text: The Caribbean rule forbids any exceptions, requiring strict adherence to Section 8 in all cases.
There is no difference; all regional application paragraphs in Chapter 8 are identical.
The Caribbean rule permits only LOAs, not directives, to prescribe different procedures.
The Caribbean rule relies on section-specific exceptions rather than separately listing directives or LOAs as superseding authorities.

items.question_text: What are the two conditions that must both be met before an IFR flight in the Miami CTA may be cleared to climb or descend in VFR conditions?
items.explanation: VFR climbs and descents in the Houston, Miami, and San Juan CTAs require two conditions: (1) pilot request, AND (2) between sunrise and sunset. One hour before sunset satisfies the time condition. The pilot has requested the climb. Both conditions are met.

choices.text: No, because the climb must be completed before noon local time.
No, because VFR climbs are only permitted in the Houston CTA, not Miami.
Yes, but only if the controller also issues alternative instructions in case VFR conditions become impractical.
Yes, because the pilot requested it and it is between sunrise and sunset.

items.question_text: When Section 9 Pacific procedures are silent on a matter, what procedures should a controller apply in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.explanation: The Pacific regional application states services are provided with Section 9 procedures and minima except when noted otherwise. When Section 9 is silent, the general Chapter 8 procedures provide the fallback framework.

choices.text: The general Chapter 8 procedures, which serve as the underlying framework when the regional section does not address a situation.
Only ICAO Pacific regional standards, disregarding Chapter 8 entirely.
The Caribbean regional procedures, because Pacific and Caribbean regions share the same rules.
The North Atlantic procedures, because they are the default for all oceanic ICAO regions.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, two aircraft on reciprocal tracks have only vertical separation available. How long must vertical separation be maintained relative to the estimated passing time?
items.explanation: For reciprocal track aircraft in the Pacific region where lateral separation is not provided, vertical separation must be provided at least 10 minutes before and after the time the aircraft are estimated to pass or are estimated to have passed. This ensures a 20-minute window of vertical separation centered on the passing point.

choices.text: Only until the aircraft have physically passed each other.
At least 10 minutes before and 10 minutes after the estimated passing time.
At least 15 minutes before and 15 minutes after the estimated passing time.
At least 5 minutes before and 5 minutes after the estimated passing time.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, what lateral separation minimum applies to aircraft operating without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval?
items.explanation: In the Pacific region, lateral separation has three tiers: 23 NM for ADS-C equipped with RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180, 50 NM for RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft where authorized, and 100 NM for all other aircraft not covered by those tiers.

choices.text: 23 NM.
50 NM.

items.question_text: How does the North American ICAO Region's application paragraph differ from the other three regional application paragraphs?
items.explanation: The North American ICAO Region application (8-10-1) does not include the phrase 'except when noted otherwise' found in the North Atlantic (8-7-1), Caribbean (8-8-1), and Pacific (8-9-1) application paragraphs. It states services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 10 without a stated exception mechanism.

choices.text: It omits the 'except when noted otherwise' language found in the other three regional application paragraphs.
It is identical to all other regional application paragraphs.
It permits directives and LOAs to supersede the section, unlike the other regions.
It applies only to supersonic aircraft, unlike the other regions.

items.question_text: What makes the North American ICAO Region's vertical separation rule unique among the four ICAO regions in Chapter 8?
items.explanation: While all four regional vertical separation paragraphs reference Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, only the North American region (8-10-2) additionally requires facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes. This reflects the unique operational need to manage the flight level/metric altitude interface in the Arctic CTA.

choices.text: It is the only one that permits use of ICAO Doc 4444 for vertical separation.
It is the only one that also requires facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
It is the only one that references Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5 for vertical separation.
It is the only one that requires a letter of agreement for vertical separation to be applied.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to nonturbojet aircraft?
items.explanation: In the North American ICAO Region, the basic time-based longitudinal minima are: 15 minutes between turbojet aircraft, Mach number technique minima where applicable, and 20 minutes between other (nonturbojet) aircraft.

choices.text: 30 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, what lateral separation applies to an RNP 4 approved aircraft operating where RNP separation is authorized?
items.explanation: In the North American region, there are three lateral separation tiers: 23 NM for ADS-C equipped aircraft with RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180 (in Anchorage Oceanic/Continental CTAs only, not Arctic CTA), 50 NM for RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft where authorized, and 90 NM for aircraft not covered by the narrower tiers.

choices.text: 50 NM.
23 NM.
90 NM.

items.instruction: Where would you find the applicable rule?
items.question_text: You are working offshore airspace and encounter a wake turbulence separation situation. Chapter 8 does not address wake turbulence criteria. Where do you find the applicable separation rule?

choices.text: Elsewhere in JO 7110.65, because offshore airspace rules state that provisions in the Order apply for situations not covered by Chapter 8.
Only in ICAO documents, because offshore airspace is governed exclusively by international standards.
In facility directives only; the Order outside Chapter 8 does not apply to offshore airspace.
Nowhere — situations not covered by Chapter 8 cannot be resolved in offshore airspace.

items.explanation: Offshore airspace rules state that Chapter 8 procedures and minima apply first, and for situations not covered by Chapter 8, the provisions in the Order must apply. This means the controller should look to other chapters of JO 7110.65 for the wake turbulence criteria.

items.instruction: Identify which area permits the described operation.
items.question_text: Match each VFR flight plan operation to the area where it is permitted: (1) Operations at FL 190, (2) Operations 80 NM seaward from the shoreline, (3) Operations in airspace below the Oceanic CTAs in any FIR. Which area permits operations 80 NM seaward within controlled airspace?

choices.text: The Miami, Houston, and San Juan CTAs below FL 180.
The Oakland FIR, when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace.
All Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs.
All three areas permit operations at 80 NM seaward.

items.explanation: The Oakland FIR permits VFR flight plan operations when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace. The CTAs limit altitude (below FL 180) rather than distance. The all-FIR airspace category applies below Oceanic CTAs.

items.instruction: Identify the correct exception trigger.
items.question_text: An oceanic control area has an en route chart depicting a different altimeter setting basis for a specific route. The facility also has a written directive prescribing local altimeter settings for another route. A controller is working a third route with no charted or directed exception. Which routes use 29.92 inches Hg and flight levels?

choices.text: Only the directed route uses an exception; charted procedures cannot override the 29.92 standard.
All three routes use 29.92, because charted and directed exceptions do not override the oceanic standard.
Only the third route with no exception — the charted route and the directed route use their respective exceptions.
All three routes use the charted setting, because charted procedures always take precedence everywhere.

items.explanation: The oceanic altimeter rule uses flight levels and 29.92 inches Hg unless directed and/or charted otherwise. Both directed and charted exceptions are recognized triggers. The third route with no exception follows the default 29.92 rule.

items.instruction: Select the statement that correctly describes the reportable oceanic error categories.
items.question_text: Which list correctly identifies the five reportable oceanic error categories under JO 7210.632?

choices.text: Transponder failures, CPDLC logon rejections, altitude deviations, speed deviations, and route deviations.
Lateral deviations, vertical deviations, speed deviations, communication failures, and surveillance failures.
Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time errors, speed errors, and communication failures.
Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations verification.

items.explanation: JO 7210.632 establishes procedures for reporting five categories: Gross Navigation Errors (GNE), height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations (SAO) verification. The other lists contain items not found in the source.

items.instruction: When must control estimates be used?
items.question_text: A controller is applying both time-based and distance-based separation between oceanic aircraft. For which type of separation are control estimates required to be used?

choices.text: Only for time-based separation minima.
For both time-based and distance-based separation minima.
Control estimates are optional for all separation types.
Only for distance-based separation minima.

items.explanation: Control estimates are the estimated position of aircraft with reference to time. They must be used when applying time-based separation minima. For distance-based separation, other position references apply.

items.instruction: Select the statement that correctly describes the RVSM exception rules.
items.question_text: Which statement correctly describes the rules for non-RVSM aircraft in oceanic RVSM airspace?

choices.text: Supervisor approval alone is sufficient for any non-RVSM aircraft to enter RVSM airspace.
Supervisor approval alone, without meeting the excepted aircraft criteria, is sufficient for any non-RVSM aircraft to enter RVSM airspace.
Non-RVSM exceptions are mandatory requirements that the controller must accommodate regardless of current workload or traffic conditions.
A non-RVSM aircraft that meets the excepted criteria may enter RVSM airspace without any further approval or coordination.

items.explanation: The RVSM exception rule has three layers: (1) the aircraft must meet the criteria of an excepted aircraft, (2) prior supervisor/CIC approval is required, and (3) exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis. All three layers apply.

items.instruction: Identify which ARTCC coordination duty is being described.
items.question_text: An ARTCC forward's a flight's updated estimate from 1345Z to 1349Z to the receiving oceanic facility. Which coordination duty is the ARTCC performing?

choices.text: Forwarding current flight plan and control information.
Coordinating with adjacent ATS facilities when the airspace to be protected will overlap the common boundary.
Forwarding to appropriate ATS facilities the current flight plan and control information as the flight progresses.
Coordinating IFR/VFR service continuation.

items.explanation: The 4-minute revision meets the 3-minute-or-more threshold, so this action falls under the duty to forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more to the appropriate ATS facility.

items.instruction: Identify the correct rule for the situation.
items.question_text: Nonradar separation is being applied. You are transferring an oceanic flight to the next facility. The aircraft is estimated to reach the boundary at 1535Z. By what time must air-ground communications be transferred?

choices.text: 1545Z, 10 minutes after the boundary crossing.
1535Z, at the time of boundary crossing.
1525Z, 10 minutes before the estimated boundary crossing.
1530Z, 5 minutes before the estimated boundary crossing.

items.explanation: Where nonradar separation minima are applied, communications transfer must occur 5 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to reach the boundary, unless otherwise agreed.

items.instruction: Which minimum applies?
items.question_text: Two aircraft on the same track require different time-based longitudinal standards. Aircraft A, a turbojet using Mach number technique, is subject to a 10-minute minimum. Aircraft B, a nonturbojet, is subject to a 30-minute minimum. Which minimum applies between them?

choices.text: 30 minutes, the larger of the two standards.
10 minutes, the smaller standard applicable to the lead aircraft.
15 minutes, the general Chapter 8 default.
20 minutes, the average of the two standards.

items.explanation: When one aircraft requires a different time-based longitudinal standard than another, apply the larger of the two standards between the aircraft concerned. The more restrictive minimum protects both aircraft.

items.instruction: Put the reciprocal track separation steps in the correct order.
items.question_text: For aircraft on reciprocal tracks, what is the correct sequence for applying longitudinal separation?

choices.text: Wait for both aircraft to report passing each other, then apply vertical separation retroactively.
Wait for both aircraft to report passing each other visually or by reference to ground-based aids, then apply vertical separation retroactively.
Apply the crossing-track minimum at the point of intersection; reciprocal tracks use the same rule as crossing tracks.
Apply the same-direction longitudinal minimum for the entire crossing without using vertical separation, because reciprocal tracks and same tracks use identical separation rules.

items.explanation: Reciprocal track separation requires: (1) vertical separation before and after estimated passing for the applicable minimum interval, then (2) vertical separation may be discontinued only after aircraft have reported passing a significant point AND the same-direction minimum is achieved.

items.instruction: Where would you find the applicable lateral separation minimum?
items.question_text: A controller is working an aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region and needs to verify the applicable lateral separation minimum for that region. Where in Chapter 8 should the controller look?

choices.text: Section 1, General, which contains all lateral separation minima.
Section 4, Lateral Separation, which applies uniformly to all regions.
Section 7, North Atlantic ICAO Region.
Section 10, North American ICAO Region, which is the default for all regions.

items.explanation: The note in 8-4-1 states that lateral separation minima are contained in the regional sections: Section 7 (North Atlantic), Section 8 (Caribbean), Section 9 (Pacific), and Section 10 (North American-Arctic CTA). The controller should look in the relevant regional section.

items.instruction: Identify which method establishes lateral separation.
items.question_text: Two aircraft are on nonintersecting flight paths. The protected airspace of their flight paths does not overlap, and reduced route protected airspace is applicable. Does lateral separation exist?

choices.text: Yes, but only if the aircraft are at least 50 NM apart.
No, nonintersecting flight paths always require the full required distance between flight paths.
No, lateral separation for nonintersecting paths requires radar identification of both aircraft.
Yes, when reduced route protected airspace is applicable and the protected airspace does not overlap.

items.explanation: For nonintersecting flight paths, lateral separation exists either when the required distance is maintained between flight paths OR when reduced route protected airspace is applicable and the protected airspace does not overlap. The second method provides an alternative to the full distance requirement.

items.instruction: Compare the two altitude-based reduction standards.
items.question_text: Compare the route protected airspace reduction rules below FL 240 versus at and above FL 240. Which values are correct for the reduced width on each side of centerline?

choices.text: Below FL 240: 5 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 10 miles each side.
Below FL 240: 10 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 5 miles each side.
Below FL 240: 10 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 15 miles each side.
Below FL 240: 5 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 5 miles each side — there is no difference.

items.explanation: Below FL 240, protected airspace reduces to 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID. At and above FL 240, it reduces to 10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles. Both then increase on a 5-degree angle. Higher altitude = wider reduced protection.

items.instruction: Identify the pressure-setting risk addressed below FL 180.
items.question_text: Why must controllers account for QNE and QNH differences below FL 180 when aircraft cross the offshore/oceanic boundary?

choices.text: Above FL 180, vertical separation is never applied across the offshore/oceanic boundary.
At and above FL 180, all aircraft use the standard altimeter setting of 29.92, so there is no QNE/QNH difference to manage.
Below FL 180, all aircraft use local altimeter settings that differ from the oceanic standard.
Below FL 180, aircraft are always in controlled airspace requiring special handling.

items.explanation: The transition level in oceanic airspace is typically FL 180. At and above this level, all aircraft use QNE (29.92 inches Hg / standard pressure setting). Below FL 180, aircraft may be using local QNH settings. The rule ensures the difference between QNE and QNH does not erode vertical separation.

items.instruction: Identify both timing conditions.
items.question_text: An outbound aircraft in offshore-to-oceanic transition needs to climb through an opposite-direction oceanic aircraft. Vertical separation must be established before the outbound crosses the boundary. What is the other timing requirement?

choices.text: Vertical separation must be established 30 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Vertical separation must be established 10 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Vertical separation must be established 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
No additional timing requirement exists beyond the boundary crossing condition.

items.explanation: Both timing conditions must be met: vertical separation must be established (a) before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary, AND (b) 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

items.instruction: Determine whether the same-direction climb-through procedure is authorized.
items.question_text: A following aircraft requests a same-direction climb through the preceding aircraft's altitude. The preceding aircraft is level and faster, 5 minutes spacing exists, the following is 3,000 ft below, and the following will begin climb 8 minutes after the preceding's reporting point. Direct communication is maintained. May the controller use the procedure?

choices.text: No. The following aircraft must wait exactly 10 minutes after the reporting point.
No. The altitude difference must be exactly 4,000 feet.
No. The preceding aircraft must be slower than the following aircraft.
Yes. The scenario satisfies all five conditions for the same-direction climb-through procedure.

items.explanation: All five conditions are met: (a) preceding is level and equal or greater speed, (b) 5 minutes maintained, (c) following not more than 4,000 ft below (3,000 ft satisfies this), (d) climb begins within 10 minutes after the reporting point (8 minutes satisfies this), (e) direct communication maintained.

items.instruction: Determine whether the procedure is authorized.
items.question_text: A turbojet at Mach 0.84 is transitioning from oceanic airspace into radar coverage at high speed. The controller estimates insufficient time to establish another form of separation if radar identification is delayed. May the radar identification transition procedure be applied?

choices.text: No, the procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish other approved separation if radar ID is delayed.
Yes, the procedure applies automatically whenever an aircraft enters radar coverage from oceanic airspace.
No, because the procedure requires the transiting aircraft to be radar identified before crossing the boundary.
Yes, but only for aircraft at or below FL 290.

items.explanation: The radar identification transition procedure has an explicit exception: it is not authorized if there is insufficient time for the controller to establish other approved separation in the event of a delay or inability to establish radar identification, considering factors such as aircraft performance, speed, weather, traffic, workload, and frequency congestion.

items.instruction: Choose the correct separation method.
items.question_text: A controller needs to separate an aircraft from a temporary stationary airspace reservation along a route where the protected airspace would overlap the reservation. How can the aircraft still transit the area?

choices.text: Cancel the aircraft's clearance and reroute to a completely different airway.
Cancel the aircraft's IFR clearance and instruct the pilot to reroute to a completely different airway that avoids the reservation area entirely.
No separation method works; aircraft must always be rerouted around stationary reservations.
Use longitudinal separation by measuring the time interval from the reservation activation to the aircraft's estimated crossing point.

items.explanation: Two methods are available for stationary reservations. If lateral separation is unavailable (protected airspace would overlap), vertical separation may be used. The protected area for vertical separation includes the reservation plus a buffer of one-half the lateral separation minimum.

items.instruction: Order the controller actions when a pilot refuses an avoidance clearance.
items.question_text: A pilot refuses a clearance to avoid an active airspace reservation. Put the controller's required actions in the correct sequence.

choices.text: Inform the using agency first, then advise the pilot of the hazard, then issue a new clearance.
Approve the refusal, request the pilot's intentions, then inform the using agency.
Inform the pilot of the potential hazard, then advise that services will not be provided in the reservation, then if possible inform the using agency.
Request the pilot's intentions, then inform the pilot of the hazard, then approve or deny the refusal.

items.explanation: The three actions are: (1) inform the pilot of the potential hazard, (2) advise that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation, and (3) if possible, inform the appropriate using agency. The third is conditional but should be attempted.

items.instruction: Identify which separation method matches the description.
items.question_text: For a temporary moving airspace reservation, which method clears aircraft so that the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation?

choices.text: Vertical separation.
Lateral separation.
Radar separation.
Longitudinal separation.

items.explanation: Moving reservations have three separation methods: lateral (non-overlapping protected airspace), longitudinal (minimum ahead of first or behind last aircraft in the reservation), and vertical (reservation plus perimeter buffer). The longitudinal method treats the reservation as a moving block in time/distance.

items.instruction: Where would you confirm the regional rules apply?
items.question_text: A controller is working an aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region and needs to confirm that Section 7 lateral separation minima apply rather than the general Chapter 8 lateral minima. Where is the governing rule that establishes Section 7 as the controlling authority?

choices.text: Section 1 General provisions, because the general rules in Chapter 8 apply uniformly across all ICAO regions regardless of the regional sections.
The lateral separation note, which lists the regional lateral minima but does not establish which section controls for a given aircraft in the region.
ICAO Document 7030, the North Atlantic Regional Supplementary Procedures, which operates independently of JO 7110.65 for this region's airspace.
Section 1, General, which applies uniformly to all regions regardless of regional provisions.

items.explanation: Each regional section has an application paragraph that establishes the section as the governing authority for that region. For the North Atlantic, 8-7-1 states that services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 7 except when noted otherwise.

items.instruction: Where would you find the vertical separation rules?
items.question_text: You need to verify vertical separation procedures for an aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region. Where should you look?

choices.text: ICAO Doc 4444, Procedures for Air Navigation Services.
Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
Chapter 8, Section 7, North Atlantic ICAO Region only.
Chapter 8, Section 5, Offshore/Oceanic Transition Procedures.

items.explanation: All four regional vertical separation paragraphs (8-7-2, 8-8-2, 8-9-2, 8-10-2) reference Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification as the source for vertical separation procedures.

items.instruction: Match each aircraft type to its longitudinal minimum.
items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, which longitudinal minimum matches which aircraft category? Match: (A) Supersonic, same type cruise climb with common point — (B) Nonturbojet outside WATRS — (C) Turbojet subsonic not using Mach technique.

choices.text: A = 10 minutes, B = 20 minutes, C = 10 minutes.
A = 15 minutes, B = 20 minutes, C = 10 minutes.
A = 10 minutes, B = 30 minutes, C = 15 minutes.
A = 15 minutes, B = 30 minutes, C = 15 minutes.

items.explanation: In the NAT Region: supersonic same-type cruise climb with common point = 10 minutes; nonturbojet outside WATRS = 30 minutes; turbojet subsonic not using Mach technique = 15 minutes. Each category has a distinct minimum reflecting the aircraft's navigation and speed characteristics.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum.
items.question_text: An RNP 4 approved aircraft with CPDLC and ADS-C contracts maintained is operating in airspace designated for 23 NM lateral separation in the North Atlantic ICAO Region. What lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 60 NM.
50 NM.
30 NM.
23 NM.

items.explanation: 23 NM applies to approved aircraft (minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180) operating within designated airspace with direct controller/pilot communications via voice or CPDLC, and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.

items.instruction: Where would you confirm the regional rules?
items.question_text: A controller working in the Caribbean ICAO Region encounters a situation where Section 8 procedures differ from general Chapter 8 procedures. Which source confirms that Section 8 controls?

choices.text: The Caribbean application paragraph, which states services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 8 except when noted otherwise.
The general Chapter 8 rule, because it applies uniformly over all regional sections.
The ICAO Caribbean Regional Supplementary Procedures.
The Caribbean lateral separation section only; the vertical and longitudinal sections defer to Chapter 8 generally.

items.explanation: The application paragraph for each ICAO region establishes that the region-specific section controls for that area. For the Caribbean, 8-8-1 states that services are provided with Section 8 procedures and minima except when noted otherwise.

items.instruction: Identify the correct longitudinal minimum for each aircraft type.
items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, match: (1) supersonic aircraft of the same type in cruise climb with common point, (2) nonturbojet aircraft in the WATRS, (3) turbojet aircraft below FL 200 in the San Juan Oceanic CTA outside WATRS.

choices.text: 1 = 10 minutes, 2 = 15 minutes, 3 = 30 minutes.
1 = 10 minutes, 2 = 20 minutes, 3 = 20 minutes.
1 = 15 minutes, 2 = 20 minutes, 3 = 20 minutes.
1 = 15 minutes, 2 = 30 minutes, 3 = 15 minutes.

items.explanation: Caribbean longitudinal minima: supersonic same-type with common point = 10 minutes; nonturbojet in WATRS = 20 minutes; turbojet below FL 200 in San Juan (outside WATRS), Miami, and Houston CTAs/FIRs = 20 minutes. Note that 20 minutes appears for multiple categories.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum for the described operation.
items.question_text: Two aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval are operating west of 55 degrees West between the U.S. and Bermuda. What Caribbean lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 90 NM.
60 NM.

items.explanation: In the Caribbean, 90 NM applies to aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 operating within WATRS, or west of 55 West between the U.S./Canada/Bermuda and Caribbean points. The 100 NM tier applies to other aircraft west of 55 West not covered by narrower tiers. The 120 NM tier applies east of 55 West.

items.instruction: What must the controller do?
items.question_text: An IFR flight in the San Juan CTA has been cleared to climb in VFR conditions. The controller notices weather deteriorating along the route, and VFR conditions may become impractical. What must the controller do?

choices.text: Cancel the IFR flight plan and have the aircraft continue VFR.
Nothing; the pilot is responsible for maintaining VFR conditions.
Immediately recall the clearance and instruct the aircraft to return to its previously assigned altitude.
Issue alternative instructions, and issue traffic information to aircraft not separated per the section minima.

items.explanation: When an IFR flight is cleared for VFR climb or descent in the CTAs, and there is a possibility that VFR conditions may become impractical, the controller must: (1) issue alternative instructions, and (2) issue traffic information to aircraft not separated in accordance with the minima in the section.

items.instruction: Where is the governing rule?
items.question_text: You are providing ATC service in the Pacific ICAO Region. A colleague says general Chapter 8 procedures always apply. Where do you confirm that Section 9 procedures take precedence for this region?

choices.text: The Pacific application paragraph, which states services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 9 except when noted otherwise.
The general oceanic rule in Section 1, which states directives and LOAs may prescribe different procedures.
The ICAO Pacific Regional Supplementary Procedures; JO 7110.65 does not independently govern the Pacific Region.
Chapter 4, which is the universal source for all oceanic airspace procedures.

items.explanation: The Pacific application paragraph (8-9-1) establishes that ATC services in the Pacific ICAO Region are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 9 except when noted otherwise. It is the regional governing rule.

items.instruction: Match the minimum to the situation.
items.question_text: Which Pacific longitudinal minimum applies when turbojet aircraft follow the same track or continuously diverging tracks until another form of separation is provided?

choices.text: 20 minutes.
10 minutes.
5 minutes.
15 minutes.

items.explanation: In the Pacific Region, the time-based longitudinal minima include: 15 minutes between aircraft (general), 10 minutes between turbojet aircraft on same/continuously diverging tracks until another form of separation is provided, and Mach number technique minima.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum.
items.question_text: An RNP 10 approved aircraft is operating in the Pacific ICAO Region in an area where RNP separation is authorized. CPDLC is not currently available and ADS-C contracts are not maintained. What lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 23 NM.
50 NM.
30 NM.

items.explanation: 50 NM applies to RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft in areas where RNP separation is authorized. The 23 NM tier additionally requires CPDLC/voice communications and ADS-C contracts maintained with automated flight data processing. Without CPDLC and ADS-C, the aircraft qualifies for 50 NM based on RNP 10 approval.

items.instruction: Identify the key difference.
items.question_text: Compare the four regional application paragraphs. The North Atlantic, Caribbean, and Pacific paragraphs all contain four words not found in the North American application paragraph. What are they?

choices.text: 'Contained in this section.'
'With the procedures and minima.'
'Provide air traffic control services.'
'Except when noted otherwise.'

items.explanation: The North American application paragraph omits 'except when noted otherwise' — language present in all three other regional application paragraphs. The North American region simply states services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 10 without a stated exception mechanism.

items.instruction: What makes the North American vertical separation unique?
items.question_text: What additional document beyond Chapter 4, Section 5 is required for vertical separation in the North American ICAO Region?

choices.text: Facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
A letter of agreement with the Anchorage ARTCC.
No additional document is required; the North American rule is identical to other regions.
An ICAO regional air navigation agreement.

items.explanation: The North American vertical separation paragraph is unique in requiring both Chapter 4, Section 5 AND facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes. This reflects the operational need to manage flight level/metric altitude interfaces in the Arctic CTA.

items.instruction: How long may DME/RNAV separation continue without direct voice communications?
items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, two turbojet aircraft are being separated using DME/RNAV in the NOPAC Route System. Both are radar identified. One aircraft's voice communications become unreliable. How long may DME/RNAV separation continue?

choices.text: Not more than 60 minutes without direct voice communications.
Not more than 90 minutes without direct voice communications.
DME/RNAV separation requires direct voice communications at all times and cannot continue without it.
Indefinitely, because both aircraft are radar identified.

items.explanation: Per the North American DME/RNAV separation rules, unless both aircraft are radar identified, both must provide DME/RNAV distance reports via direct voice. Application without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum.
items.question_text: An aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval is operating in the North American ICAO Region outside the Anchorage Oceanic and Continental CTAs. What lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 23 NM.
50 NM.
90 NM.

items.explanation: The North American region has three lateral tiers: 23 NM (ADS-C with RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180 in Anchorage Oceanic/Continental CTAs only), 50 NM (RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved where authorized), and 90 NM (aircraft not covered by the narrower tiers).

items.front: What must a controller do when a situation in offshore airspace is not covered by Chapter 8?
items.back: Apply the provisions elsewhere in JO 7110.65 that address the situation. Chapter 8 applies first, but the rest of the Order fills gaps.

items.front: What is the altitude limit for VFR flight plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs?
items.back: Below FL 180. Operations above FL 180 in these CTAs are not permitted on a VFR flight plan.

items.front: Under what condition may radar separation be used as one of the required separation types in oceanic/offshore procedures?
items.back: Radar separation may be used where radar coverage is adequate, as specified in Chapter 5, Radar.

items.front: What two circumstances permit departure from the flight levels and 29.92 inches Hg standard for altitude assignment in oceanic control areas?
items.back: When directed otherwise, or when charted otherwise. Both directives and charted procedures may prescribe a different altimeter setting basis.

items.front: Does the 10-minute action deadline apply to ALL missed position reports in oceanic airspace?
items.back: No. The deadline applies only when the position report affects separation. If a missing report does not affect separation, the 10-minute action requirement does not apply.

items.front: For which type of separation must control estimates be used?
items.back: Control estimates must be used when applying time-based separation minima. They provide the estimated position of aircraft with reference to time.

items.front: What three independent requirements must be satisfied before a non-RVSM aircraft may operate in oceanic RVSM airspace?
items.back:
1. Meet the criteria of an excepted aircraft (initial delivery, maintenance ferry for failed RVSM, mercy/humanitarian, or spare-engine-under-wing in specific FIRs).
2. Previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC.
3. Accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis.

items.front: What is the minimum estimate revision that ARTCCs must forward to the receiving ATS facility?
items.back: Revisions of 3 minutes or more. Revisions of less than 3 minutes do not require forwarding.

items.front: Under what condition does the nonradar 5-minute communications transfer rule NOT apply as written?
items.back: When the control and/or communication units concerned have agreed otherwise. The 5-minute rule applies 'unless otherwise agreed.'

items.front: What two conditions must both be met before vertical separation may be discontinued for aircraft on reciprocal tracks?
items.back:
1. Both aircraft have reported passing a significant point (or passing ground-based NAVAIDs/DME fixes indicating they have passed each other).
2. The aircraft are separated by at least the applicable same-direction longitudinal minimum.

items.front: What is the operational purpose of the Mach number technique?
items.back: It allows for the application of reduced longitudinal separation minima by ensuring aircraft maintain assigned Mach numbers, enabling more accurate prediction of relative positions.

items.front: For intersecting flight paths with constant but different width protected airspace, how is the applicable lateral separation minimum determined?
items.back: It is the sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths, measured perpendicular to the flight path of the other aircraft. Each aircraft's protected airspace width is added together.

items.front: Compare the reduced route protected airspace below FL 240 versus at and above FL 240. What are the widths and distances?
items.back: Below FL 240: 5 miles each side to 57.14 miles from NAVAID. At/above FL 240: 10 miles each side to 114.29 miles. Both then expand on a 5-degree angle. Higher altitude = double the width and distance.

items.front: Compare the minimum divergence angles for track separation: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN vs NDB vs Dead Reckoning.
items.back:
VOR/VORTAC/TACAN: 15 degrees minimum divergence.
NDB: 30 degrees minimum divergence, and one aircraft at least 15 miles from the NAVAID.
Dead Reckoning: 45 degrees minimum divergence, and one aircraft at least 15 miles from the intersection point.

items.front: What operational risk does the altitude/flight level transition rule below FL 180 address?
items.back: The risk that differences between standard altimeter setting (QNE, 29.92) and local altimeter setting (QNH) could compromise vertical separation when aircraft cross the offshore/oceanic boundary.

items.front: What are the two timing conditions for an outbound aircraft to climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic during offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.back:
1. Vertical separation established before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary.
2. Vertical separation established 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

items.front: What are the first three conditions for the same-direction 5-minute climb-through procedure in offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.back:
1. Preceding aircraft is level at assigned altitude with speed equal to or greater than the following.
2. Minimum of 5 minutes maintained between preceding and following.
3. Following aircraft separated by NOT MORE THAN 4,000 feet from preceding when climb clearance issued.

items.front: What are the final two conditions for the same-direction 5-minute climb-through procedure in offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.back:
4. Following aircraft commences climb within 10 minutes after passing the exact reporting point or radar-observed position of the preceding.
5. Following aircraft is in direct communication with ATC until vertical separation is established.

items.front: When using vertical separation from a temporary stationary airspace reservation, how is the protected area defined?
items.back: The stationary reservation PLUS a buffer around the perimeter equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

items.front: What is the governing rule for ATC services in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: Services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 7 of Chapter 8, except when noted otherwise. Section 7 procedures take precedence over general Chapter 8 for this region.

items.front: What is the source for vertical separation procedures in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification. This is the same source referenced by all four ICAO regional vertical separation paragraphs.

items.front: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what are the basic longitudinal minima for each aircraft category not using Mach number technique?
items.back:
Supersonic (different types/cruise): 15 minutes.
Turbojet subsonic: 15 minutes (or Mach technique).
Nonturbojet in WATRS: 20 minutes.
Nonturbojet outside WATRS: 30 minutes.

items.front: What are the three narrowest lateral separation tiers in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back:
1. 23 NM — approved aircraft (min RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180) with CPDLC/voice and ADS-C.
2. 50 NM — RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved in New York/San Juan/Atlantic Miami CTAs/FIRs.
3. 60 NM or 1° latitude — supersonic above FL 275, or MNPS/NAT HLA aircraft.

items.front: What are the two widest lateral separation tiers in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back:
4. 90 NM or 1.5° latitude — non-RNP aircraft on WATRS/San Juan/Atlantic Miami routes, or U.S./Canada-Bermuda-Caribbean routes west of 55°W.
5. 120 NM or 2° latitude — all other aircraft not covered by the narrower tiers.

items.front: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to turbojet aircraft at or above FL 200 versus below FL 200?
items.back: At or above FL 200 in listed CTAs/FIRs: 15 minutes (or Mach technique). Below FL 200 in San Juan (outside WATRS), Miami, and Houston CTAs/FIRs: 20 minutes. The minimum doubles below FL 200 for turbojets in these areas.

items.front: How does the Caribbean lateral separation scheme differ east versus west of 55 degrees West?
items.back: West of 55°W: non-RNP aircraft 90 NM; others not covered by narrower tiers 100 NM. East of 55°W: 120 NM for aircraft not covered by narrower tiers. The eastern Caribbean has a wider base lateral minimum.

items.front: What two conditions must both be satisfied before an IFR flight may be cleared to climb or descend in VFR conditions in the Houston, Miami, or San Juan CTA?
items.back:
1. The climb or descent must be requested by the pilot.
2. It must be between sunrise and sunset.
Both conditions must be met; neither alone is sufficient.

items.front: In the Pacific ICAO Region, what vertical separation window is required for reciprocal track aircraft without lateral separation?
items.back: Vertical separation must be provided at least 10 minutes before and after the time the aircraft are estimated to pass or are estimated to have passed. This creates a minimum 20-minute vertical separation window centered on the passing point.

items.front: What makes the North American ICAO Region application paragraph unique compared to the other three regional application paragraphs?
items.back: It is the only regional application paragraph that does NOT include the phrase 'except when noted otherwise.' It states services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 10 without a stated exception mechanism.

items.front: What are the two sources that govern vertical separation in the North American ICAO Region?
items.back:
1. Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
2. Facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
This is the only regional section that requires facility directives as a second source.

items.front: In the North American ICAO Region, what are the time-based longitudinal minima for each aircraft category?
items.back:
Turbojet: 15 minutes (or Mach technique).
Other (nonturbojet): 20 minutes.
Simpler than other regions — only two basic time-based categories.
